name: São Paulo
description: São Paulo is Latin America's largest city and a crucible of modernist and brutalist architecture. From Niemeyer's sinuous curves to Lina Bo Bardi's bold public spaces, the city's concrete jungle hides some of the most culturally vibrant buildings in the Southern Hemisphere.

images.alt: São Paulo skyline with brutalist and modernist buildings

name: Salvador
description: Salvador da Bahia, Brazil's first capital, is where colonial Portuguese architecture meets Afro-Brazilian culture. Its Pelourinho district is a UNESCO World Heritage Site, and the city's layered history is inscribed in every facade.

images.alt: Salvador da Bahia colorful colonial architecture

name: Kanazawa
description: Kanazawa is one of Japan's best-preserved Edo-era cities, where samurai districts, geisha quarters, and Kenroku-en garden coexist with bold contemporary architecture. SANAA's 21st Century Museum has made it a pilgrimage site for architecture lovers worldwide.

images.alt: Kanazawa cityscape blending traditional and contemporary architecture

name: Lausanne
description: Lausanne is a hillside city on Lake Geneva, home to EPFL — one of Europe's leading technical universities — and an emerging hub of contemporary architecture. The city's steep topography creates dramatic relationships between buildings and the Alpine landscape.

name: New York
description: New York City is the world's most iconic skyline and a living encyclopedia of architectural history — from cast-iron SoHo to Art Deco midtown to the glass towers of Hudson Yards. Every era of architecture has left its mark on Manhattan's relentless grid.

images.alt: New York City skyline with diverse architectural styles

name: Lens
description: Lens is a post-industrial city in northern France that has been reborn through culture. The arrival of the Louvre-Lens museum on the site of a former coal mine has transformed this working-class town into an unexpected destination for art and architecture.

images.alt: Lens cityscape with post-industrial and cultural architecture

name: New Canaan
description: New Canaan, Connecticut is a quiet suburb that became an unlikely laboratory for modernist domestic architecture. Philip Johnson's Glass House, the Noyes House, and now SANAA's Grace Farms make it one of America's most architecturally significant small towns.

images.alt: New Canaan Connecticut modernist architecture amid New England landscape

name: Leça da Palmeira
description: Leça da Palmeira is a coastal town near Porto where Álvaro Siza created his earliest and most celebrated work — the Piscinas de Marés tidal swimming pools. This modest Atlantic-facing settlement became a pilgrimage destination for architects seeking Siza's primal dialogue between concrete and ocean.

images.alt: Leça da Palmeira Atlantic coastline with rocky shore

name: Porto
description: Porto is the birthplace of the 'Porto School' of architecture, led by Álvaro Siza and Eduardo Souto de Moura. The city's dramatic Douro River gorge, azulejo-clad facades, and modernist interventions create one of Europe's most photogenic and architecturally layered urban landscapes.

images.alt: Porto cityscape with Douro River and layered architecture

name: Lisbon
description: Lisbon is a sun-drenched capital of seven hills where Manueline, Pombaline, and contemporary architecture overlap. The Expo 98 district along the Tagus River showcases Portugal's modern architectural ambitions, anchored by Siza's iconic Portuguese Pavilion.

images.alt: Lisbon cityscape with historic and modern architecture on hills

name: Porto Alegre
description: Porto Alegre is the capital of southern Brazil's Rio Grande do Sul state, set on the banks of the Guaíba River. Known for its European-influenced culture, the city gained international architectural recognition with Álvaro Siza's sculptural Iberê Camargo Foundation.

images.alt: Porto Alegre waterfront with Guaíba River

name: Toužim
description: Toužim is a small Bohemian town in the western Czech Republic, surrounded by rolling farmland and forests. An unlikely destination for architectural pilgrimage, it is home to John Pawson's Nový Dvůr Monastery — a minimalist Trappist retreat that draws visitors from around the world.

name: London
description: London is a palimpsest of architectural history — from medieval churches and Georgian terraces to high-tech towers and converted industrial spaces. The city's constant reinvention makes it one of the world's richest architectural landscapes, where every street tells a different century's story.

images.alt: London skyline with historic and modern architecture

name: Osaka
description: Osaka is Japan's second city and a sprawling commercial metropolis where Tadao Ando forged his austere concrete language amid the chaos of dense urban fabric. The city's energy and contradictions — neon excess alongside meditative minimalism — have shaped some of modern architecture's most radical residential experiments.

name: Naoshima
description: A small island in Japan's Seto Inland Sea transformed into one of the world's most extraordinary art destinations. Tadao Ando's museums, embedded into the island's hillsides, anchor a landscape where contemporary art, architecture, and nature merge into a singular pilgrimage experience.

name: Venice
description: Venice is an architectural palimpsest floating on water — Byzantine, Gothic, and Renaissance layers coexist in a city where every building negotiates with the lagoon. The Biennale and adaptive reuse of historic structures keep Venice at the center of contemporary architectural discourse.

name: Kobe
description: Kobe is a port city compressed between mountains and sea, where the 1995 earthquake prompted a wave of reconstruction that attracted Japan's finest architects. Tadao Ando's works here exploit the dramatic tension between narrow waterfront sites and panoramic views of the Inland Sea.

name: Neuss
description: Neuss is a Rhine-side city near Düsseldorf, home to the Museum Island Hombroich — a former NATO missile base transformed into a landscape of art and architecture. Tadao Ando's Langen Foundation anchors this unique cultural park where Cold War relics meet contemplative gallery spaces.

name: Baku
description: Baku is a city of contrasts where the medieval walled Old City meets a 21st-century skyline of ambitious cultural projects. Zaha Hadid's Heydar Aliyev Center epitomizes the capital's rapid architectural transformation, positioning Azerbaijan at the intersection of Eastern and Western design ambitions.

name: Rome
description: The Eternal City is a living museum of architectural history spanning three millennia — from the Pantheon's concrete dome to Zaha Hadid's MAXXI. Rome's dense stratification of ancient, baroque, and contemporary layers makes every new intervention an act of negotiation with the weight of history.

name: Guangzhou
description: Guangzhou is southern China's commercial powerhouse, where rapid urbanization has attracted international architects to design landmark cultural facilities along the Pearl River. Zaha Hadid's Opera House anchors the Zhujiang New Town district, signaling the city's ambition to rival Beijing and Shanghai as a cultural capital.

name: Weil am Rhein
description: A small German town on the Swiss border that became an unlikely architectural pilgrimage site thanks to the Vitra Campus — an open-air museum of buildings by Hadid, Gehry, Ando, Herzog & de Meuron, and SANAA. Hadid's Fire Station here launched her career from paper architecture to built reality.

name: Vals
description: A remote Alpine village in the Swiss canton of Graubünden, Vals would be known only for its thermal springs were it not for Peter Zumthor's legendary bathhouse. The Therme Vals has made this tiny settlement a global destination for architects seeking the most sensory building of the late 20th century.

name: Cologne
description: Cologne is a Rhineland city defined by its Gothic cathedral and the scars of wartime destruction. The city's reconstruction created space for bold contemporary insertions, including Zumthor's Kolumba Museum, which bridges Roman ruins, medieval fragments, and modern art in a single building.

name: Mechernich
description: A small town in the Eifel region of western Germany, Mechernich is home to Peter Zumthor's Bruder Klaus Field Chapel — a rammed-earth tower standing alone in agricultural fields. The chapel has made this quiet rural landscape an essential stop on any serious architectural tour of Europe.

name: Bregenz
description: Bregenz is a lakeside Austrian city on the eastern shore of Lake Constance, known for its floating opera stage and Peter Zumthor's Kunsthaus — a glass cube that has become one of the most celebrated small museums in Europe. The city's Alpine setting and cultural ambition belie its modest size.

name: Vardø
description: Vardø is Norway's easternmost town, a remote Arctic fishing settlement on the Barents Sea. Peter Zumthor and Louise Bourgeois's Steilneset Memorial, commemorating 17th-century witch trials, has placed this windswept outpost on the global architectural map.

name: Hamburg
description: Hamburg is Germany's great port city, where the massive HafenCity redevelopment is transforming former docklands into a new urban quarter. Herzog & de Meuron's Elbphilharmonie, crowning a historic warehouse with a glass wave, has become the city's defining landmark and one of Europe's most celebrated concert halls.

name: Beijing
description: Beijing's architectural landscape spans from the Forbidden City's imperial geometry to the audacious structures of the 2008 Olympics. The Bird's Nest stadium and CCTV Headquarters signaled China's arrival as a patron of the world's most ambitious contemporary architecture.

name: Madrid
description: Madrid's architectural identity ranges from Hapsburg austerity and Bourbon grandeur to bold contemporary interventions along the Paseo del Prado. Herzog & de Meuron's CaixaForum and Jean Nouvel's Reina Sofía extension have reinvigorated the city's museum mile with provocative 21st-century forms.

name: Miami
description: Miami is a subtropical metropolis where Art Deco heritage meets explosive contemporary development. The city's waterfront museums and cultural district, anchored by the Pérez Art Museum, showcase architecture's response to climate, sea-level rise, and the energy of Latin American cultural exchange.

name: Miami Beach
description: Miami Beach is home to the largest collection of Art Deco architecture in the world and an increasingly ambitious program of contemporary building. Herzog & de Meuron's 1111 Lincoln Road proved that even a parking garage can become a cultural landmark on this barrier island.

name: Paris
description: Paris is the city where modern architecture was both born and resisted — from Le Corbusier's villas to Piano and Rogers's Centre Pompidou. The tension between Haussmann's classical order and radical contemporary insertions continues to make Paris one of the world's most architecturally charged capitals.

name: Basel
description: Basel is Switzerland's cultural capital, home to Herzog & de Meuron's headquarters and an extraordinary concentration of museums, including the Fondation Beyeler and Vitra Campus nearby. The city's commitment to architectural quality has made it a living laboratory for contemporary Swiss design.

name: San Francisco
description: San Francisco's architecture reflects its history of booms and reinventions — Victorian painted ladies, earthquake-resilient engineering, and a growing collection of green buildings. Renzo Piano's California Academy of Sciences in Golden Gate Park exemplifies the city's commitment to sustainable design.

name: Poissy
description: A quiet suburb northwest of Paris, Poissy is known to architects worldwide as the site of Le Corbusier's Villa Savoye — the single most influential house of the modern movement. The pilotis-raised white box in its meadow setting remains a place of architectural pilgrimage nearly a century after completion.

name: Ronchamp
description: A small town in the Franche-Comté region of eastern France, Ronchamp is synonymous with Le Corbusier's chapel of Notre-Dame du Haut — a sculptural masterpiece that broke with the rationalism of his earlier work. The hilltop chapel draws tens of thousands of architectural pilgrims each year.

name: Marseille
description: France's oldest city and largest Mediterranean port, Marseille is a gritty, multicultural metropolis where Le Corbusier built his most influential housing project — the Unité d'Habitation. The city's raw energy and diverse heritage continue to attract architects experimenting with social and urban innovation.

name: Éveux
description: A small commune near Lyon, Éveux is home to Le Corbusier's Sainte Marie de La Tourette — a Dominican monastery that is among the most powerful sacred buildings of the 20th century. The austere concrete structure above rolling meadows continues to function as an active religious community.

name: Cambridge
description: Cambridge, Massachusetts is home to Harvard and MIT — two universities whose campuses constitute an open-air museum of modern architecture, from Aalto's Baker House to Saarinen's MIT Chapel. Le Corbusier's Carpenter Center remains the only building by the master on North American soil.

name: Chandigarh
description: Chandigarh is the only city in the world designed entirely by Le Corbusier — a planned capital for the Indian state of Punjab built from scratch in the 1950s. Its Capitol Complex, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, remains the most monumental realization of modernist urban planning.
